summary: Gruber shares a Perl script he wrote for converting text to title case, which he uses to standardize Daring Fireball's Linked List headlines. The script goes beyond naive capitalize-every-word approaches by handling small words, preserving intentional capitalization like 'iTunes', skipping domain names, and dealing with edge cases like 'AT&T'. He acknowledges the code is ugly but functional, having grown organically over years of use. He finally publishes it despite its convoluted appearance, reasoning that working code trumps pretty code.
key_points: Naive title-casing (capitalizing every word) fails because small words like 'if', 'in', 'of' should remain lowercase
The script preserves intentionally capitalized words like 'iTunes' and skips words containing dots like domain names
Edge cases like 'AT&T' and 'Q&A' required hard-coded hacks because they contain small words that would normally be lowercased
The first and last words are always capitalized, and small words after colons get capitalized
The code grew ugly over time as edge cases accumulated, which delayed its publication for years
money_quotes: It's pretty easy to write a non-clever title-casing function. The simplest way is to just capitalize the first letter of every word. That's not right, though.

The script assumes that words with capitalized letters other than the first character are already correctly capitalized. This means it will leave a word like "iTunes" alone, rather than mangling it into "ITunes" or, worse, "Itunes".
It's one of those pieces of code that started small and simple, and grew ugly over time as edge cases were worked around one at a time.
I've been using this script for years, but have put off publishing it on the grounds that it looks like the sort of punctuation-riddled code that gives Perl phobics the heebie-jeebies.
But it works, so screw it, here it is.
key_insight: Proper title casing is deceptively complex, requiring accumulated edge-case handling that makes elegant code impossible — but working ugly code beats unpublished perfect code.
